item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite the age of this article, I do think that a little critique here can help it in the event of it needing some rewrite in the future. First of all, it does have quite interesting concepts, so it is certainly a good first SCP, demonstrated as something that can endure, and I think it can be really better.

Ultimately I downvoted, for some issues that I saw. First are several nitpicks, like the -LatAm MTF, the use of Memetics when it could/should be an Infohazard (I'll come back to this later), or the excess of unnecessary blackboxing. Second, is the heavy basis on Meso-American culture without actually diving into any of it. Third is that, in the end, it is so broad and generic that I leave with the sense that it just turns Revival religions into an SCP with some Nationalism thrown into it. Not a good feeling.

For this to work, I think that it could be reduced in scope and really delve into the developments of a couple aspects of it. Read the guides about Cognito/Info/Memetic-Hazards not only to get a better grasp of what they do, but also of what you can do deeper here, decide a direction of exploration for the anomaly and go weirder through there.

Some suggestions of mine, and observations on nitpicks:

Memetic conundrum
– hide block

As I said, this isn't a Memetic effect. The main point for this is that only the infected serve as vectors, so it is safe to learn the information through secondary means. In a memetic effect, a non-anomalous information triggers an anomalous response in the person to whom it clicks, while here the information must be anomalous to work (coming from an infected source, specifically), and it works even on people it can't affect (they all become carriers).

Cognitohazards, on the other hand, work as soon as they are perceived through the right senses. So the actual information, and therefore its comprehension, is of little importance on such an effect. Lets say it really was a Cognitohazard, people looking at a pamphlet would be affected even without reading or even being able to read the pamphlet. As it is now, it is an Infohazard, since it is the information by itself (the existence of this political entity as thought by an infected) that is anomalous, and the same info coming from a non-anomalous source have no effect. Even to the point where this information can cause anomalous alterations to affected media.

With this in mind, choosing what to keep can have a huge impact on the potential of your SCP. As an Infohazard it can have further effects on the information inside the minds of its carriers, or at least in the affected media. It can start to further describe this government, its structure, members, current location. I would make it in a way that older documents start to deviate from what they described initially, but became more like each other in the process, as if the true information blossomed there.

Memetic effects can be triggered by non-anomalous sources, and can alter perception and thinking of affected people. Keep in mind that amnestics should be effective on those almost always (while it isn't the case for the other two). A memetic effect could cause the infected to perceive several other things occurring around and linked to the Government in Exile, like interaction with envoys, embassies, and even passages to Mictlán. Those effects can be hallucinatory, real or mixed.

If you choose Cognitohazard, then one interesting option could be the documents imparting "the true understanding of Nahuatl". In that case, the person looking at the original document develops three symptoms: they learn several words in Nahuatl suddenly, if they didn't knew them before; they start to actually read Nahuatl, but maybe seeing only the anomalous propaganda (believes to know Nahuatl but do not really know); and develop false memories of how they learned Nahuatl as one learns any foreign language. In this option the information of the text can be convincing for those due to the new memories, but not anomalous by itself, and even people who do not speak the other language in the pamphlet would be able to "read" the Nahuatl passages.

Not-separatist movements
– hide block

Linking your SCP to the three groups you mentioned was an interesting move, but in doing so only to mention them as separatist groups you demonstrated a total lack of knowledge about them. Neither of them are this, and each of them is an unique thing of its own that you can use on your article.

Nuwaubian Nation is a cult, despite the name. They have never declared or requested sovereignty over any place or territory. You could use them to reinforce the subtle Fifthist theme, with their heavy emphasis on fake-Egyptology and UFOs, although they are also black supremacists (for real, they say Whites are war-bred mutants and Asians cursed with idiocy), so this can be used, but demands some tact.

Yamato Damashi is a controversial term in Japan, meaning something akin to "Japanese Spirit" (but not quite), that saw heavy use as an ultra-nationalist warcry and is commonly linked to nationalistic sentiment associated with old Japan. So it gets a lot of the "old religion" and "old country" feeling of your SCP, but it is oriented to a nation that still exists.

Bobo Ashanti is a group inside the Rastafari religion that lives a very strict life in their Utopian village near Kingston, Jamaica. They promote the idea of the African Diaspora, like other Rastafarian groups, and otherwise live like their equivalent to the American Amish People. So the Bobo do a lot for the theme of strict religion, and maybe even the idea of "New-Old" faith, and have the curious twist of considering themselves exiled.

UN-black-boxing
– hide block

Having that many black-boxes isn't helping your article, but you can actually create information to put in their place that will improve this a lot. Things like the language, the names of the leaders, groups that allegedly give support, can become the seed for furthering this and linking this in a meaningful way to something else happening or to the Foundation-verse as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, is this how this songthis song came to be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to say that I honestly could never get a feeling for Fifthism until I saw it tied to Aztec mythology in this entry. Well done. Now it makes sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of the Fifth Sun was first by a couple thousand years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding pretty much what Roget said, and the Fifthist thing seems completely shoehorned.

item.title: Pretty nice!
item.content:encoded:
As a new member to the site (Not new to the actual site, just joined, though.) I find this very well done! Nice job! And keep up the wonderful work!

It seems that with more stuff like this, you'll be with the guys who can write SCP's as fluently as Gears, Kondraki and Clef!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

Frankly, I'm amazed it has survived its first 24 hours.

I'll do some changes to it tomorrow, hopefully I can get the quality up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of article I like to see from new authors. A bit rough around the edges, but a well-executed and interesting concept nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I have an unlimited appetite for imaginary geography…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the same way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really do it for me. I like that you've got a lot of local culture worked in, and I love the idea of the Fifthists reflected in an ancient religion.

Unfortunately, as MrWrong said, there really isn't much going on here story-wise. You mention the old religions, to some extent the horrific history of colonial wars and genocide, and strange things like the leadership retreating to the land of the dead, but you can't rely on the readers here to know the details and implications of that history and mythology on their own. I know from my limited exposure that that stuff can be quite creepy, but I think it would help if you directly described some of that in the article directly.

Also, for the memetic effect, I'm not disappointed that it's similar to Samothrace like some, I'm just disapointed that not much is done with it. It causes people to spread info, and you then give a small amount of the info, and that's about it.

Final note: "Additional funding is requested to further investigate."
I don't see funding ever being the limiting factor in investigating an SCP, especially an uncontained memetic one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just feels like Mexican Samothrace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm getting a bit weary of the kind of SCPs where it's a memetic belief in the existence of some territory/government/group. Samothrace was cool and well-executed, but each one since then has been a bit less good.

This article is just dull now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Level 4 Access required.

Shouldn't the personnel assigned to this SCP have access to the kind of documents they are supposed to be containing? Level 4 is broad need-to-know access to site-wide, regional and strategic intelligence, held by Site-Directors, Security Directors, and MTF Commanders. See http://www.scp-wiki.net/security-clearance-levels

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the Fifthist vibes and the role religion in general plays in it, but the huge similarities to Samothrace make me border on downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with this, Samothrace immediately came to mind when I started reading this. I think there might be ways to separate this from it, if it focuses more on the protests it suggests or an actual effect in the calls for the mesoamerican gods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is conceptually similar to the islamic republic of eastern samothrace, but I prefer that article. This one didn't keep my interest, and while the addendum is cool, it didn't do enough for me. I'm not sure what you could do to make the article better for this issue. I didn't catch any fifthism references until I came down to the comments. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still dig it.

item.title: Re: Thanks
item.content:encoded: Actually not what I was suggesting. The subtleness works well here.

item.title: Thanks
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback, I'll try to put the clown suit of fifthism into some more nahua weirdness and make another addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Admittedly, I was "meh" until the last addendum. It tells a lot, and I would like to see more of the pamphlets/documents advocating said government in exile.

The Fifthist connection is quite subtle. One could miss it entirely, but it's alright.

EDIT: Narrative-wise, there's relatively little of it. The article describes what the government-in-exile does, but how does it affect the real world. It appears to be affecting the real world for a long time, so seeing the effects may strength the article. e.g. You mentioned it had a marriage alliance with Emperor Maximilian. How about said emperor being affected and made to believe he was marrying an Aztec princess… And then, you may build up all the pieces throughout history towards the Fifthist end-game.

EDIT EDIT: Removed my upvote and hoping for update

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can only see vague outlines of the 5thist Church, but this is actually pretty good.

item.title: SCP-2155
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP, I did my best to avoid common pitfalls for new authors.

Ideas while writing:

1- Write what you know. I know my country and some stuff about it's history is just plain creepy.

2- Try not to do something clichéd. Although there's already a memetic SCP about a third world political entity (Samothrace), there's not too many of them and I hope this is different enough to warrant inclusion.

3- I tried to make a connection between the some disturbing images in real-life mesoamerican culture and religion and put a fifthist spin on it.

A lot of credit goes to sirpudding who offered valuable pointers in the forum.
